coredata.dc:title: pH response mechanism of bifunctional fluorescent carbon quantum dots and application in cancer detection and bioself-targeting imaging
coredata.dc:creator: Hu, Xiaoyan
Zhang, Yafei
Li, Hao
Cao, Jun
Pan, Jiaqi
Li, Chaorong
Zheng, Yingying
coredata.dc:description: The development of fluorescent probes suitable for lysosome visualisation as well as real-time tracking is crucial because lysosomes play a crucial role in numerous metabolic activities and diseases. In this paper, nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs) enriched with a large number of hydroxyl, amino, and carboxyl groups were prepared by a simple solvothermal method using tartaric acid and 4-aminosalicylic acid as raw materials. The fluorescence intensity of NCQDs decreased with increasing pH (1–13) due to protonation and deprotonation as well as hydrolysis of surface groups in alkaline environment and detachment of chromophores. This enabled the NCQDs to achieve label-free and rapid visualisation of pH in cells. The disparity in pH levels between cancer cells and normal cells resulted in varying fluorescence brightness of NCQDs observed in these two cells, which enabled the differentiation of cancer cells from normal cells. Most importantly, when NCQDs entered the cell, lysosomes could provide protons for NCQDs, and the protonation of NCQDs' surface groups led to stronger fluorescence after endocytosis of NCQDs in lysosomes. Thus, lysosomal targeted imaging could be achieved without the need to introduce lysosomal-targeted ligands, and NCQDs had a higher biocompatibility and stability compared to commonly used commercial dyes, which made it the ideal choice of lysosomal-targeted fluorescent probes.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
pH-response
Fluorescent probes
Lysosomal targeting
Cellular imaging

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the Natural Science Foundation of China ( Nos. 51603187 ) and the cooperative project funding of Zhejiang Sci-Tech University with Hangzhou Mingshi Culture Dissemination co., LTD (“Application of strong fluorescence graphene quantum dots in heavy metal ion detection and cell imaging ”, No. 2022330001004838, Internal No.22060862-J ).

This work was supported by the Natural Science Foundation of China (nos. 51603187) and the cooperative project funding of Zhejiang Sci-Tech University with Hangzhou Mingshi Culture Dissemination co. LTD (“Application of strong fluorescence graphene quantum dots in heavy metal ion detection and cell imaging”, No. 2022330001004838, Internal No. 22060862-J).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The process of NCQDs preparing, its application for monitoring pH changes and lysosomal targeting ability.
(a) Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image of NCQDs. (b) Particle size distribution of NCQDs. (c) High-resolution TEM image of NCQDs. (d).
(a) FT-IR spectrum of NCQDs. (b) XPS full spectrum of NCQDs. (c) C1s fitting spectra. (d) N1s fitting spectra. (e) O1s fitting spectra.

(a) UV–vis absorption spectra of NCQDs and Photographs of NCQDs (inset) under daylight conditions and UV light irradiation. (b) Fluorescence spectra of NCQDs excitated at different wavelengths. (c) Fluorescence lifetime plot of NCQDs.

(a)Fluorescence spectra of NCQDs in different pH buffers. (b) Boltzmann fit of NCQDs in different pH buffers (R2 : Boltzmann fit correlation coefficient). (c) Linear relationship between fluorescence intensity of NCQDs and pH 1–6. (d) Linear relationship between fluorescence intensity of NCQDs and pH 8–13 (x: pH value Y: fluorescence intensity, R2 : the correlation coefficient of linear fit).

(a-c) fluorescence spectra of NCQDs under different pH conditions (pH=3, 7, 10) excited by different wavelengths (360–420 nm). (The insets on lower right showed photographs of NCQDs under UV irradiation and sunlight). (d) Reversible fluorescence response between pH 1 and 13.
High-resolution XPS spectra of C1s, O1s and N1s under different pH conditions.
(a-c) Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) of NCQDS at different pH (3–10).

(a) The confocal images of L929 cells incubated with the NCQDs at different pH (3, 5, 7, 10) values. (b) Corresponding quantitative analysis of cellular fluorescence intensities in the (a) graph. (c) Intracellular pH linear curves constructed from average fluorescence intensities of the pH images.
Fluorescence imaging of L929 cells and B16F10 cells incubated with the NCQDs.

(a) The confocal images of B16F10 cells under co-incubated with LTRs and NCQDs. (b) Distribution of fluorescence intensity of NCQDs and LysoTracker Red (LTR) at the positions marked by the white line segments in the cell (a). (c) Fluorescence intensity correlation plots of NCQDs (green channel) and LTR (red channel).

head.ce:title: pH response mechanism of bifunctional fluorescent carbon quantum dots and application in cancer detection and bioself-targeting imaging

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Optical Field Manipulation of Zhejiang Province, Department of Physics, Zhejiang Sci-Tech University, Hangzhou 310018, People's Republic of China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Optical Field Manipulation of Zhejiang Province

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Optical Field Manipulation of Zhejiang Province, Department of Physics, Zhejiang Sci-Tech University, Hangzhou 310018, People's Republic of China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The development of fluorescent probes suitable for lysosome visualisation as well as real-time tracking is crucial because lysosomes play a crucial role in numerous metabolic activities and diseases. In this paper, nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs) enriched with a large number of hydroxyl, amino, and carboxyl groups were prepared by a simple solvothermal method using tartaric acid and 4-aminosalicylic acid as raw materials. The fluorescence intensity of NCQDs decreased with increasing pH (1–13) due to protonation and deprotonation as well as hydrolysis of surface groups in alkaline environment and detachment of chromophores. This enabled the NCQDs to achieve label-free and rapid visualisation of pH in cells. The disparity in pH levels between cancer cells and normal cells resulted in varying fluorescence brightness of NCQDs observed in these two cells, which enabled the differentiation of cancer cells from normal cells. Most importantly, when NCQDs entered the cell, lysosomes could provide protons for NCQDs, and the protonation of NCQDs' surface groups led to stronger fluorescence after endocytosis of NCQDs in lysosomes. Thus, lysosomal targeted imaging could be achieved without the need to introduce lysosomal-targeted ligands, and NCQDs had a higher biocompatibility and stability compared to commonly used commercial dyes, which made it the ideal choice of lysosomal-targeted fluorescent probes.

ce:list-item.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The development of advanced fluorescence detection tools for dynamic observation of subcellular organelle morphology and physiological evolution in live cell imaging plays a key role in unravelling life processes and the mechanisms behind them [1–4]. Lysosomes, as acidic intracellular organelles (pH 4.0–6.0) containing a variety of hydrolytic enzymes, are closely associated with a wide range of cellular activities, and play an essential role in protein catabolism, signalling, skeletal remodelling, autophagy, and repair of the plasma membrane [5–9]. Abnormal changes in lysosomes may lead to a variety of health problems. It has been reported that apoptosis due to gradient decay of lysosomal protons leads to acidification of the cytoplasm and an increase in lysosomal pH which may lead to diseases such as Alzheimer's disease and even cancer. Therefore, accurate localisation of lysosomes and precise determination of lysosomal pH in living cells on this basis are essential for a deeper understanding of physiological and pathological mechanisms [10–15].

By using a single fluorescent probe that specifically targets lysosomes, it is possible to capture images of lysosomes and keep track of their ever-changing positions and shapes during physiological activities [16,17]. However, most of the current commercial probes for lysosomes have the disadvantages of low specificity for localisation and poor adaptability for long-time detection, as well as low photostability and poor biocompatibility, which are not conducive to long-term monitoring of lysosomal functional and structural dynamic changes [18,19]. Moreover, most fluorescent dyes, especially organic fluorophores, are highly susceptible to photobleaching due to irreversible photodamage in their internal structure. Although several quench-resistant seals and reducing agents have been developed for fixed and living cells, these methods are relatively complex in terms of practical steps [20]. In addition, in some cases, these fluorescent dyes may be reduced to more toxic substances, which is not conducive to the long-term monitoring of lysosomal functional and structural dynamics and may be detrimental to lysosomal function and structure. Therefore, fluorescent labelling materials with higher resistance to photobleaching, high stability and high biocompatibility show great potential for future fluorescence imaging applications.

Carbon quantum dots (CDs) with a size of less than 10 nm are quasi-spherical nanoparticles with excellent photoluminescence properties, good water solubility, long-lasting photostability, low cytotoxicity, and good biocompatibility, which make them stand out among many fluorescent nanomaterials [21–26].CDs can be tailored to meet specific needs such as cancer diagnosis, cancer therapy, and subcellular organelle localisation by passivation of the surface of the CDs, doping of the CDs with heteroatoms, or modification of the CDs with functional molecules/groups, which makes them more widely used in biomedical fields [27–32]. For example, Marković et al. prepared fluorine (F)- and chlorine (Cl)-doped CQDs, which were doped with a larger transport bandgap, higher charge transfer resistance, and better antioxidant properties [33].Shinde et al. prepared a hydrophilic LRCDs, which showed a strong PTT effect using near-infrared radiation, indicating that the LRCDs could be used as a promising photothermal therapeutic agent for the effective treatment of cancer by fluorescence imaging [34].

After screening the raw materials, nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots(NCQDs) with organelle-targeting properties could be prepared. Given that lysosomes are acidic organelles in cells and lipophilic amines are concentrated more than 100 times in lysosomes than in other organelles, the precursors should be enriched with a large number of hydroxyl and amino groups, which would allow the preparation of NCQDs with pH-sensitive properties and intrinsic lysosomal-targeting ability [35]. In this paper, we chose hydroxyl‑rich tartaric acid and amino-containing 4-aminosalicylic acid as the reaction materials in order to design a carbon dot that could be used for lysosome-targeted imaging. And then nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots with green fluorescent properties were successfully prepared by a solvent method. The surfaces of our prepared NCQDs were enriched with a large number of hydroxyl and amino groups and exhibited highly sensitive properties to pH. Through cellular imaging experiments, we found that the NCQDs had excellent cytocompatibility and were capable of lysosome-targeted imaging without the additional addition of lysosome-targeted ligands. With an absolute fluorescence quantum yield of about 10.14 %, it exhibited excellent photostability, selectivity, and low toxicity, enabling label-free and fast visualisation of pH in cells. Furthermore, the contrast in pH between healthy cells and cancer cells allowed us to differentiate them. Taken together, the NCQDs were expected to be ideal for high-resolution pH imaging of cells and organisms and showed great potential for applications in cell biology (Scheme 1 ).

4-Aminosalicylic acid (98 %) and tartaric acid (≥95 %) were purchased from Shanghai McLean Biochemical Co. Dulbecco's Modified Eagle Medium/Nutrient Mixture F-12 medium (DMEM/F12) and 1640 medium was procured from Hunan Fenghui Biotechnology Co., Ltd. CellCountingKit-8 (CCK8) was obtained from Beyotime Biotechnology Inc., Nantong, China. The penicillin-streptomycin solution and foetal bovine serum were attained from Invitrogen, USA, and PBS buffer (for cell culture) was acquired from Beijing Lanjieke Technology Co. LysoTracker Red was bought from Biyuntian Biological Co.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM JEM-2100) and atomic force microscope (Bruker Dimension ICON) were used to transmit the microscopic appearance and lattice fringes of NCQDs. Quantum yields of NCQDs were measured with a bulk spectrometer fluorescence (FluoroMax-4). The UV–vis absorption spectra were got using a Hitachi U-3900 spectrophotometer. The fluorescence spectra of NCQDs were measured by Hitachi U-3900 spectrophotometer. X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) of NCQDs were measured on a Kratos Axis Ultra X-ray photoelectron spectrometer. Fourier Transform Infrared (FTIR) spectra were tested using a Nicolet iS50 FTIR Spectrometer. Zeta potential was determined using a Malvern Zetasizer Pro. Fluorescence imaging was done on a laser confocal microscope (Olympus Fv-1000, Japan) system.

Carbon quantum dots were synthesised by a simple solvothermal method. Firstly, tartaric acid and 4-aminosalicylic acid were dispersed in deionised water, followed by 10 min of ultrasonication, and finally the treated solution was transferred to a Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave (50 mL). Next, the autoclave was placed in an oven, heated to 200 °C and the reaction was carried out for 8 h. When the reactor was cooled to room temperature, the product was centrifuged (9000 rpm for 10 min). The supernatant was taken and purified by placing it in a dialysis bag (MW: 500 Da) for 48 h of dialysis. Next, a 0.22 μm microporous membrane was used to filter out the large particles and then the solution was collected for freeze-drying and finally the collected powder was stored in a dark environment for later use.

NCQDs solution (100ug/ml) was obtained by dissolving NCQDs in deionised water. The NCQDs solution was diluted to 50ug/ml in PBS buffer of different pH. The fluorescence intensity of NCQDs in different pH was tested at an excitation wavelength of 390 nm. All the spectroscopic tests were done at room temperature. The reversibility of the fluorescence response of NCQDs to pH was investigated by using hydrochloric acid solution and sodium hydroxide solution to repeatedly adjust the pH between 1 and 13.

L929 and B16F10 cells were purchased from Hunan Fenghui Biotechnology Co. L929 cells were cultured in 1640 culture medium containing 10 % FBS and 1 % penicillin-streptomycin, with 5 % CO2 content in the cell culture incubator at 37 °C. The culture medium for B16F10 cells was DMEM/F12, which also contained 10 % FBS and 1 % penicillin-streptomycin, and the incubator conditions were the same as those described above.

Firstly, B16F10 and L929 cells were digested for 1 min using 0.25 % trypsin to obtain a single cell suspension. Next, these cells were inoculated into 96-well plates at a concentration of 5000/well. The NCQDs were uniformly dispersed into DMEM/F12 pure medium according to a concentration gradient of A 25 μg/mL, B 50 μg/mL, C 75 μg/mL, D 100 μg/mL, E 125ug/ml, and F 150ug/ml. Subsequently. After the cells were completely attached to the walls of the petri dishes, the mixed NCQDs solution was added to 96-well plates and placed in a cell culture incubator to co-culture with the cells for 12 h. The viability of the cells was assessed using the CCK-8 method.

L929 cells were inoculated in 15 mm dishes and incubated in a 5 % CO2 incubator for 12 h until the cells were completely attached to the walls of the dishes. After that, the medium was removed and L929 cells were co-incubated with a mixture of NCQDs solution (100ug/ml) and medium for 3 h. After that, the medium containing NCQDs was removed and L929 cells were rinsed twice with PBS buffer to remove extracellular NCQDs. L929 cells were then incubated in the medium of different pH for 8 min before fluorescence imaging were taken with a laser confocal microscope. B16F10 cells were co-incubated with NCQDs solution (100ug/ml) under the above conditions for 3 h. The medium containing NCQDs was removed and B16F10 cells were rinsed with PBS buffer for 2 times before fluorescence imaging were taken by laser confocal microscopy. Lysosomal co-localisation experiments were also performed under the above conditions, where B16F10 cells were co-cultivated with NCQDs (100ug/ml) for 3 h before the medium containing NCQDs was removed and B16F10 cells were rinsed twice with PBS buffer to remove extracellular NCQDs. Afterwards, a red lysosomal probe, LysoTracker Red (LTR), was added to the petri dish for 10 min of incubation, and then the cells were washed 3 times with PBS buffer to prevent free lysosomal probes from interfering with the imaging results. Fluorescence imaging was performed immediately on a laser confocal microscope.

Nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs) were successfully synthesised by solvothermal method using tartaric acid and 4-aminosalicylic acid as the reaction raw materials. In order to obtain the best properties of NCQDs, the effects of synthesis conditions, such as raw material ratio (the mass ratio of tartaric acid and 4-aminosalicylic acid =1:1, 1:2 or 4:5), reaction duration (6 h, 8 h, 10 h), reaction temperature (160 °C, 200 °C, 240 °C) and different solvents (water, ethanol, ethylene glycol) were investigated. The NCQDs synthesised under different conditions were tested by UV–vis absorption spectra and fluorescence spectra, and the results were shown in Figs. S1 and S2. From Figs. S1 and S2, it could be observed that the UV absorption and fluorescence properties were all optimal synchronously when the reaction temperature was 200 ℃, the reaction time was 8 h, the solvent was water, and the raw material mass ratio was 4:5. Therefore, the final synthesis conditions were determined: tartaric acid and 4-aminosalicylic acid were set in a mass ratio of 4:5 with water as solvent and the reaction were conducted at the temperature of 200 °C for 8 h. Tartaric acid and 4-aminosalicylic acid were dehydrated and carbonised at high temperatures to form graphene-like flakes, and single- or multi-layer graphene-like flakes were stacked to form a "core", with oxygen and nitrogen functional groups on the surface to form a "shell", which resulted in the formation of NCQDs with a core-shell structure.

The morphology of the NCQDs was characterised by using transmission electron microscopy (TEM). As demonstrated in Fig. 1 a, the NCQDs exhibited a near-spherical shape and showed a monodispersed morphology, with an average particle size of about 2.31 nm ±0.1 nm (Fig. 1b). From High-resolution TEM (Fig. 1c and d), it could be seen that the spacing of the lattice stripes of NCQDs was 0.29 nm, which was corresponding to the (020) crystal plane of graphene. A broad diffraction peak about 26°was observed in the XRD pattern of NCQDs (Fig. 1e), which verified that the NCQDs had a graphite-like structure [36–38].
The highlighted area of c. (e) XRD pattern of NCQDs.

The surface functional groups of NCQDs were characterised by Fourier infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy. The results were shown in Fig. 2 a, where the characteristic peak at 3380 cm−1 was caused by the NH stretching vibration, while the characteristic peak at 3240 cm−1 was generated by the OH stretching vibration, and the characteristic peaks at 3070 cm−1 and 2920 cm−1 were caused by CH stretching vibrations on the benzene ring and alkanes respectively. The characteristic peak at 1720 cm−1corresponded to the CO stretching vibration, while the characteristic peak at 1610 cm−1 was brought by the aromatic CC stretching vibration. The peak of the CN stretching vibration was located at 1460 cm−1, while the peaks of the CC stretching vibration and CO stretching vibration were 1260 cm−1 and 1210 cm−1, respectively [39]. All of these above indicated that the prepared NCQDs were graphitic ring structures and were rich in functional groups such as amino groups, hydroxyl groups, and carboxyl groups. The elemental composition of the NCQDs was further probed by using X-ray electron spectroscopy (XPS). The full spectrum of the NCQDs (Fig. 2b) revealed three different bond energy peaks at 284.81 eV, 400.11 eV, 531.87 eV, which corresponded to C1s, N1s, and O1s, and their contents were 64.88 %, 5.12 %, and 29.99 %, respectively. The high-resolution energy spectra of the three elements were analysed. Fig. 2c demonstrated three bond energy peaks of C1s, which were located at 284.4 eV, 286 eV and 288.5 eV, respectively. These peaks corresponded to CC/CC, CO/CN, and CO/CN, respectively. Whereas, in the high-resolution energy spectra of N1s (Fig. 2d), three peaks were shown at 399.4 eV, 400 eV and 401.5 eV, which corresponded to the energy peaks of pyridine N, pyrrole N and graphite N, respectively. In the O1s spectra (Fig. 2e), the energy spectral peaks at 531.3 eV, 532.4 eV and 533.3 eV corresponded to CO, CO and OH [40,41]. The test data of XPS were in high agreement with the data of FT-IR, which both indicated that a large number of functional groups existed at the surface of NCQDs. Based on the aforementioned characterisation data, it could be confirmed that the preparation of NCQDs was successful.

In order to investigate the optical properties of NCQDs, they were characterised by UV spectrophotometry and fluorescence spectrophotometry. The UV–vis absorption spectra of NCQDs (Fig. 3 a) revealed two absorption peaks, of which the one at 273 nm was due to the π-π* jump of aromatic CC, while the one at 384 nm was closely related to the n-π *jump [42–44]. NCQDs appeared yellowish under daylight conditions and emitted bright yellow-green fluorescence under UV light irradiation (shown in the inset on the upper right corner of Fig. 3a). From the fluorescence spectrogram (Fig. 3b), it could be clearly observed that the optimal emission wavelength of NCQDs was located at 520 nm under the excitation wavelength of 390 nm, and the emission wavelength did not change with the excitation wavelength from 360 to 420 nm which was showing excitation-independent characteristics. NCQDs had a fluorescence lifetime period of 4.22 ns (Fig. 3c), and the absolute fluorescence quantum yield (QY) of NCQDs was 10.14 % (Fig. S3). To investigate the photostability of NCQDs, the variation of fluorescence intensity of aqueous solutions of NCQDs was observed at different Na+ ion concentrations and standing times. As demonstrated in Fig. S5a, the fluorescence intensity of the aqueous solution of NCQDs did not change significantly after 7 days of storage, and the formation of precipitate was also not observed. As observed in Fig. S5b, it was found that there was no significant difference in the fluorescence intensity of the aqueous solution of NCQDs under different concentration conditions of Na+ ions. All these observations indicated that NCQDs exhibited excellent stability, which were creating conditions for their further application in the biological field.

A standard optical response of NCQDs to pH was performed in PBS buffer to investigate the fluorescence intensity change to pH at the excitation wavelength of 390 nm. From Fig. 4 a, it could be observed that the change of pH resulted in the fluorescence characteristics change of NCQDs in terms of fluorescence intensity and emission wavelength. The fluorescence intensity was weakened with the increase of pH, and the emission wavelength underwent the phenomenon of blueshift from an acidic to an alkaline environment, which indicated that the prepared NCQDs had sensitive characteristics to pH. The fluorescence intensity of NCQDs showed a sharp decrease between pH 6 and 8. To comprehensively explore the pH sensitive behavior of NCQDs, the changes in fluorescence intensity of NCQDs within 6–8 range (6.2, 6.4, 6.6, 6.8 and so on) were carefully examined, which were shown in Fig. S6a. The fluorescence intensity gradually decreased with the increase of pH. From the Boltzmann fitting plots of different pH values versus the fluorescence intensity of NCQDs (Fig. 4b), it could be observed that the change of fluorescence intensity of NCQDs was relatively weak in strong acid or strong alkali environments. However, the fluorescence intensity showed a sharp decrease during the conversion from acidic to basic. From Fig. 4c and d, it was evident that there was a strong correlation between the pH and fluorescence intensity under acidic condition (pH 1–6, R2 = 0.996) or alkaline condition (pH 8–13, R2 = 0.994), respectively.

When the NCQDs solution changed from an acidic environment to an alkaline environment, the fluorescence characteristics of NCQDs changed. Firstly, the emission wavelength shifted from 520 nm in an acidic environment to 500 nm in an alkaline environment when excited by 390 nm. Second, there was only one emission peak in an acidic environment and when excited by different wavelengths (Fig. 5 a), the emission peak did not change showing the excitation-independent. However, there were two emission peaks in an alkaline environment excited by different wavelengths (from 360 nm to 420) (Fig. 5b and c). The initial emission peak was influenced by excitation, whereas the subsequent emission peak was not influenced by excitation. The first peak of NCQDs emission was at 430 nm, and the second peak was at 500 nm. Under daylight conditions, the change in pH had a weak effect on the colour of the aqueous solution of NCQDs, but under UV irradiation, the colour was gradually changed from a light yellow to a greenish colour with the increase in pH from 3 to 10 (Fig. 5a–c)). After five repeated adjustments of the pH of NCQDs solution between pH 1 and 13, their fluorescence intensity was successfully changed back to the initial state (Fig. 5d). Based on the aforementioned experimental results, NCQDs had the potential to be used as fluorescent sensors for pH. The reason for this phenomenon of the fluorescence properties changing of NCQDs with pH might be that NCQDs formed a core-shell structure during carbonation and the polymer matrix shell surrounding the carbonated nucleus could contain additional PL centres, i.e., amino groups. The presence of these groups inhibited the vibrational relaxation phenomenon and the fluorescence intensity increased when the pH was 1–7. Shell fluorescence dominated at this time. Whereas, when the pH was 8–13, these groups were hydrolysed in an alkaline environment, leading to strong non-radiative vibrational relaxation, which made the fluorescence weaker. At this time nuclear fluorescence dominated [45].

According to the literature, pH-sensitive carbon quantum dots were mainly triggered by energy fluctuations, aggregation phenomena, and protonation and deprotonation processes [46]. In order to better understand the mechanism of the responsiveness of NCQDs to pH, the zeta potential of NCQDs were tested under various pH conditions. Zeta potential results were shown in Fig. S6b, where NCQDs underwent from protonation to deprotonation process, and the zeta potential of their surfaces was altered. The zeta potential decreased with increasing pH in acidic conditions, and when NCQDs were in alkaline conditions, they showed a negative potential. In acidic environment, the surface functional groups of NCQDs were positively charged due to protonation. In alkaline environments, the surface functional groups of NCQDs, such as amino group (-NH2) and carboxyl group (-COOH), underwent deprotonation, resulting in negative potentials on their surfaces.

To gain insight into the pH-sensitive fluorescence properties, detailed X-ray electron spectroscopy (XPS) analyses were performed to determine the chemical compositions and surface groups of the NCQDs at different pHs (3, 7, 10). From the full XPS spectra, three characteristic peaks at 284 eV, 400 eV and 532 eV were observed (Fig. S7), which corresponded to C1s, N1s, and O1s bonds, respectively. When the pH was 3, the contents of elements C, N, and O were 68.6 %, 4.94 %, and 26.46 %, respectively, whereas they were 59.94 %, 2.13 %, and 37.93 % when the pH was 7, and they were 59.86 %, 2.68 %, and 37.45 % when the pH was 10 (Table S1). These data indicated that the elemental content of the material could be controlled by adjusting the pH. Under alkaline conditions, the surface functional groups of carbon quantum dots underwent decomposition, such as the hydrolysis of amide bonds to form carboxyl groups, resulting in an increase in oxygen content and a decrease in nitrogen content, which was also the reason for the change in fluorescence characteristics. The high-resolution maps of C1s, N1s, and O1s of NCQDs are shown in Fig. 6 . Three significant characteristic peaks of the C1s with different pH values were observed, which corresponded to the CC/CC, CN/CO, and CO/CN bonds, respectively. The high-resolution energy spectra of N1s at pH 3 showed three characteristic peaks, which corresponded to pyridine N, pyrrole N, and graphite N. While the high-resolution energy spectra of N1s at pH 7 and 10 presented only two characteristic peaks, which corresponded to pyridine N and pyrrole N, respectively. The reason was that the amide bonds on the surface of the carbon quantum dots underwent hydrolysis, resulting in the disappearance of the energy spectrum peak of graphite N. In the high-resolution energy spectra of O1s, three significant characteristic peaks were observed at pH 3, which corresponded to CO, CO, and OH bonds, which implied the presence of oxygen-related groups such as carbonyl and carboxyl groups in the NCQDs, respectively. However, when the pH values were 7 and 10, there were only two characteristic peaks corresponding to CO and CO, and the OH present under alkaline conditions disappeared. An in-depth study of the content of various bonds in the XPS spectra was carried out with the aim of investigating how the chemical composition and surface group of NCQDs changed in various pH environments. As the pH increased from 3 to 10, there was a reduction of CN related groups in graphite (Table S2). The pyrrole form of elemental N decreased with the deprotonation process relative to oxygen-related groups, but increased with protonation (Table S3). In addition, due to deprotonation, the carbonyl group was converted to a carboxyl group when pH reached 10 (Table S4). When pH reached 10, the amount of carboxyl groups increased (Table S4), which indicated the presence of protonation. Graphite N and OH bonds also disappeared with the deprotonation process (Tables S3 and S4). All of the above indicated that during the transition from acidic to alkaline environments, due to protonation and deprotonation, as well as hydrolysis of amides under alkaline conditions, the dissociation of chromophores such as amino groups led to a gradual decrease in the fluorescence of NCQDs and changes in surface groups and zeta potentials.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and atomic force microscope (AFM) were used to characterise the microscopic appearance of NCQDs at different pH values (3, 7, 10). The test results were shown in Figs. 7 a–c and S8a–c. At pH 3, the NCQDs particles with a spherical shape and a height of 3.34 nm were clearly visible, uniform in size and distribution. At pH 7, the NCQDs particles with a height of 2.52 nm were irregular in shape and size and were not uniform in distribution, and at pH 10, the NCQDs particles with a height of 2.31 nm were fuzzy and irregular. This might be due to the fact that with the gradual increase of pH, the surface groups of NCQDs began to deprotonate and hydrolyse in the alkaline environment, resulting in the change of their surface morphology.

Low toxicity and good biocompatibility are essential for fluorescent probes used for live cell imaging. Therefore, the cytotoxicity and biocompatibility of NCQDs were investigated using the CKK-8 assay before being used as fluorescent probes for cellular imaging. NCQDs solutions with different concentrations (0, 25, 50, 75, 100, 150 μg/mL) were used, and the cells were co-incubated with NCQDs for 12 h at 37 °C (5 % CO2). The results, as shown in Fig. S9, showed that the survival rate of both L929 and B16F10 cells was above 90 %, which indicated that the NCQDs had low toxicity as well as good biocompatibility. It also indicated that the prepared NCQDs were suitable for cellular imaging because NCQDs had little effect on cell activity.

In order to investigate whether NCQDs were suitable for monitoring pH changes in living cells, fluorescence imaging was performed after NCQDs were co-incubated with cells in different pH environments (3, 5, 7, 10). The results were shown in Fig. 8 a, from which it could be observed that the luminosity brightness among the cells gradually became darker with regards to the pH of the environment in which the cells were located, which was due to the high responsible property of NCQDs to pH. The fluorescence intensity of the cell imaging was quantitatively analysed using ImageJ software. The results showed that NCQDs had a good pH response in cells (Fig. 8b), and the correlation coefficients between the fluorescence intensities and pH were linearly fitted to reach R2 = 0.993 (Fig. 8c). These results suggested that NCQDs could be used as fluorescent probes for label-free monitoring of intracellular pH changes.

Taking advantage of the highly sensitive fluorescence response property of NCQDs to pH, NCQDs was employed to distinguish cancer cells from healthy cells through the use of fluorescence imaging. NCQDs were separately incubated with normal cells (L929) and cancer cells (B16F10) for imaging analysis. As shown in Fig. 9 , compared to normal cells, cancer cells stained with NCQDs showed brighter fluorescence intensity. This was due to the fact that cancer cells were in an acidic environment while normal cells belonged to a neutral environment, and when NCQDs entered the cancer cells, the protonation of their surface groups caused a marked increase in fluorescence intensity in cancer cells compared to normal cells, allowing NCQDs to differentiate between these two cells without the need for any other chemical treatments.

To verify the lysosomal targeting ability of NCQDs, co-localisation experiments of NCQDs with the commercial lysosomal dye LysoTracker Red (LTR) and NCQDs were performed. As shown in Fig. 10 a, there was a high overlap between the green fluorescence of NCQDs and the red fluorescence of commercial probes. Fig. 10b showed that the fluorescence intensity trends of NCQDs and LysoTracker Red (LTR) staining at the position marked by the white line segment in Fig. 10a were well overlapped with each other, and the fluorescence intensity of NCQDs (channel that was green in color) and LTR (channel that was red in color) was calculated by ImageJ software with a correlation coefficient of 87 (Fig. 10c), which indicated that NCQDs were mainly concentrated in lysosomes in endocytosis. This was due to the fact that lysosomes were acidic intracellular organelles with a pH range of 4.5–6.5, lysosomes could provide protons for NCQDs, and the protonation of NCQDs' surface groups led to stronger fluorescence after endocytosis of NCQDs in lysosomes, indicating that NCQDs could autonomously image lysosomes without the need of incorporating a lysosomal-targeting ligand, and therefore could be used as a lysosomal targeting fluorescent probe.

In summary, we have successfully prepared a kind of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs) with pH-sensitive properties by the solvothermal strategy using tartaric acid and 4-aminosalicylic acid as the precursors and have deeply investigated their response mechanisms. Under different pH (1–13) conditions, due to the deprotonation and protonation of the surface groups, the hydrolysis of surface groups in alkaline environments, and the detachment of chromophores, the fluorescence properties of NCQDs changed in terms of fluorescence intensity and emission wavelength. As pH increased, the fluorescence intensity weakened and the emission wavelength shifted blue. The NCQDs had core shell structure. In pH range (1,7) shell fluorescence was dominant, while in pH range (8,13), core fluorescence was dominant. The findings from cell imaging indicated that there were differences in fluorescence brightness under different pH conditions, which could be used to monitor changes in intracellular pH values. The disparity in pH levels between cancer cells and normal cells resulted in different fluorescence brightness of NCQDs, which enabled the differentiation of cancer cells from normal cells. Moreover, NCQDs were capable of lysosome-targeted imaging without the additional addition of lysosome-targeted ligands. Compared with commonly used commercial probes, NCQDs had higher stability and biocompatibility. Taken together, these data indicated that NCQDs had great potential and promise in the field of intracellular pH detection, lysosome-targeted fluorescent probe, and bioimaging. In addition, the surface of NCQDs was rich in hydrophilic functional groups, which was expected to serve as an ideal carrier for the subcellular organelle-targeted delivery of drugs or other biomolecules.

Xiaoyan Hu: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft. Yafei Zhang: Investigation, Data curation. Hao Li: Investigation, Data curation. Jun Cao: Validation. Jiaqi Pan: Validation. Chaorong Li: Validation. Yingying Zheng: Writing – review & editing, Resources, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare no competing financial interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Natural Science Foundation of China (nos. 51603187) and the cooperative project funding of Zhejiang Sci-Tech University with Hangzhou Mingshi Culture Dissemination co., LTD (“Application of strong fluorescence graphene quantum dots in heavy metal ion detection and cell imaging”, No. 2022330001004838, Internal No. 22060862-J).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.molstruc.2024.137939.
